BUSINESS.RISK.GLOBAL_RISK.TITLE: Global Business Risk Grade
BUSINESS.RISK.GLOBAL_RISK.TOOLTIP: Risk Grade – Standardized credit score – will be A, B, C, D, or E~~~Credit Limit – Amount of credit available at any one time~~~DBT – Days Before Term a dollar-weighted average number of days beyond the contractual due date that a business pays its bills based on tradelines that have been updated in the previous 3 months. DBT is calculated at +30 days, so a DBT of 5 means that a customer is paying, on average at 35 days after invoicing~~~Industry DBT – Industry Average Days Before Term~~~Contract Limit - A yardstick for the maximum contract capacity on a single contract over a 12-month period. This measurement views the applicant as a supplier of goods and services whereas a credit limit assesses the applicant as a purchaser~~~Payment Remarks – Summary of the overall Financial status~~~
BUSINESS.RISK.LEGAL_IMPACT.TITLE: Legal/Financial Impacts
BUSINESS.RISK.LEGAL_IMPACT.TOOLTIP: Bankruptcy (US) Contains Yes if a bankruptcy has been filed against a company in the last 9 years and 9 months~~~Liens (US) The number of tax liens against the company~~~Judgements (US) The number of judgements filed against the company~~~Currency (US) Dollar;Value (US) The total dollar amount covered by all tax lien, judgement, bankruptcy filings~~~UCC Filings (US) The number of Uniform Commerical Code filings made by the Company~~~Cautionary UCC Filings (US) The number of Cautionary Uniform Commerical Code filings made by the Company~~~Suits (US) The number of suits filed against the company

HOW.SUBCONTRACTOR.WORKS:
Another supplier has identified your company as a subcontractor to help support their work with a client.
They have invited you to connect to them and their client site as the client requires all subcontractors to be identified and be compliant.
By accepting a request, your content (General Access Data and Limited Access Data) will be shared with the inviting supplier and the client.
Once connected, the client's requirements will be passed to you and upon completion, you will be able to do work on the connected site.
New connections may result in additional charges to your account.
For more information, including specifics about General Access Data and Limited Access data, see the End User Service Agreement.

RANGE.AMBER.DESC: SMI scores in this range show lack of evidence around proactive safety management programs and may indicate reactive safety practices, focusing on incident response rather than prevention. These suppliers need training on site-specific safety plans, closer monitoring, and greater oversight for high-risk activities.
RANGE.GREEN.DESC: Higher SMI scores show evidence of more mature safety practices, effective implementation, and a strong safety culture. They highlight continuous improvement efforts and can showcase contractors as examples of strong safety management organizations.
RANGE.LIME.DESC: SMI scores in this range reflect proactive safety systems focused on accident prevention and employee engagement. While above average, sharing best practices and monitoring performance with these organizations can still drive greater improvements.
RANGE.RED.DESC: SMI scores in this range indicate minimal or absent evidence of safety practices. Rigorous inspections, detailed oversight, and monitoring are essential to mitigate potential risks and incidents for these organizations.

SMI.CATEGORY.HAZARD.CTRL.RISK: Hazard Identification, Control, and Risk Management
SMI.DESC.DRAWER: This model created by Professor Patrick Hudson, helps organizations identify where they fall on the spectrum of overall organizational maturity. As organizations move from Pathological to High Reliability, employees are increasingly engaged and there is a higher degree of trust and accountability within the organization. Each level builds on the previous one, representing a natural progression in the safety maturity journey. However, this journey is not permanent, and organizations may move between levels based on the current activities of the organization and beliefs and attitudes of employees.
SMI.DESC.MAIN: This model helps organizations identify where they fall on the spectrum of overall organizational maturity. As organizations move from Pathological to High Reliability, employees are increasingly engaged and there is a higher degree of trust and accountability within the organization.
SMI.DESCRIPTION: The Safety Maturity Index (SMI) identifies potential gaps in an organization's Safety Management System (SMS). Based on global research and standards, SMI evaluates five key areas critical to reducing injuries and accidents. Each category is scored from 0 to 100, with 100 being the highest maturity score. SMI scores help guide training, programs, and oversight strategies to improve contractor safety practices.
SMI.LEVEL.1.DESC: Safety activities at this stage are minimal. The organization does not prioritize safety and health.
SMI.LEVEL.1.NAME: Pathological
SMI.LEVEL.1.QUOTE: “What cares as long as we are not caught.”
SMI.LEVEL.2.DESC: The organization is transitioning from one that does not prioritize Safety and Health to one that responds to safety and health issues when they arise.
SMI.LEVEL.2.NAME: Pathological to Reactive
SMI.LEVEL.3.DESC: Safety efforts focus on responding to incidents after they occur. The organization investigates accidents, documents findings, and takes corrective action. Reporting systems are in place to capture what happened. This stage reflects a commitment to learning from events and preventing repeat occurrences.
SMI.LEVEL.3.NAME: Reactive
SMI.LEVEL.3.QUOTE: “Safety is important; we do a lot every time we have an incident or enforcement.”
SMI.LEVEL.4.DESC: The organization is working to build systems and processes for meeting safety and health expectations and for performing activities. Compliance remains a top priority for the organization. But safety is becoming part of normal business operations.
SMI.LEVEL.4.NAME: Reactive to Calculative
SMI.LEVEL.5.DESC: Safety is managed through established systems, written procedures, and regular performance tracking. Training programs help workers understand their responsibilities. Audits (formal reviews) verify that procedures are followed. This stage reflects a structured approach to managing safety across the organization.
SMI.LEVEL.5.NAME: Calculative
SMI.LEVEL.5.QUOTE: “We have systems in place to manage all hazards.”
SMI.LEVEL.6.DESC: Workers and leaders are beginning to collaborate on identifying hazards before incidents occur. The organization is creating channels for employees to share observations and suggestions. The system is evolving from a focus on what can be documented and audited to one that is more integrated into the day-to-day and adapts to organizational realities.
SMI.LEVEL.6.NAME: Calculative to Proactive
SMI.LEVEL.7.DESC: Safety efforts focus on identifying and addressing hazards before they lead to incidents. Workers are encouraged to report near-misses (close calls that could have caused harm) and potential concerns. Leadership actively supports continuous improvement—an ongoing effort to find better ways of working. Communication about safety flows openly between all levels of the organization.
SMI.LEVEL.7.NAME: Proactive
SMI.LEVEL.7.QUOTE: “Safety leadership and values drive continuous improvement”
SMI.LEVEL.8.DESC: The company is on the path from Proactive to High Reliability. Safety is becoming a shared value that guides daily decisions. Trust between workers and leadership continues to strengthen. The organization is building habits of continually learning and adapting together. The organization is pursuing safety and health because it is good business, not because it is a requirement of the job.
SMI.LEVEL.8.NAME: Proactive to Generative
SMI.LEVEL.9.DESC: High Reliability Organization—a workplace where safety is woven into the way business is conducted. High Reliability Organizations have a preoccupation with failure, a reluctance to simplify, deference to expertise, sensitivity to operations, and commitment to resilience. Safety is the key to operating a business with high levels of performance and success. Open reporting and learning from experience are standard practices. Workers at all levels feel comfortable raising concerns. The organization continuously learns, adapts, and improves. This level reflects a mature safety culture where protecting people is central to how work is performed every day.
SMI.LEVEL.9.NAME: Generative
SMI.LEVEL.9.QUOTE: “Health and Safety is fully integrated into our business”
